old_claim.note: Radiology Departments provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: Urban sanitary surveys inspect population health and environmental conditions; clinical X-ray departments are neither part of that method nor chronologically available for the documented 1838 inquiries.

source_shape.source_locator: Prefatory letter (pp. iii-viii) dates metropolitan sanitary inquiries to 1838; Appendix instructions 1-4 (pp. xi-xvi) define their methods as disease returns, local reports, personal inspection, and examination of housing, drainage, refuse, streets, and sewerage.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The primary record documents urban sanitary inquiry methods in 1838 without diagnostic imaging.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A practice operating in 1838 cannot depend on radiology departments established only after the 1895 discovery of X-rays, and the documented survey workflow concerns environmental inspection and population records rather than clinical imaging.

ontology_before: The node incorrectly linked environmental and population-health surveys to a later hospital imaging institution.
ontology_after: Radiology remains in the clinical-diagnostics lineage and is absent from the direct boundary of sanitary surveys.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-urban-sanitary-surveys-boundary: urban_sanitary_surveys no longer directly depends on radiology_departments.
would_reject_if: The node is explicitly rescoped to a post-1896 survey method whose defining workflow requires radiological imaging.
short_reason: The documented sanitary inquiries predate X-rays and use environmental inspection and health records, not radiology.
